title: Your first Go program 🐹
instruction:
Welcome! You just picked one of the fastest, most practical languages in tech.

Go powers Docker, Kubernetes, Uber's backend, and Twitch. Companies love it because it's simple, fast, and just works.

The code below is already written — just press **Run** and watch what happens.
successMessage: Boom — you just ran a Go program. That's it. No setup, no config files, no drama. Let's keep going.

title: Make it personal
instruction:
See the text inside the quotes? That's what the computer says out loud.

Think of `fmt.Println()` as a megaphone — whatever you put between the quotes, it shouts to the screen.

Change `"Hello, World!"` to your name and run it.
hint: Only change the text between the quotes. Leave everything else exactly as it is.

codeChecks.message: Replace "Hello, World!" with your name — keep the quotes!

successMessage: Nice — the computer now knows your name. Well, sort of. It just does what you tell it. Which is the whole point.

title: Say more stuff
instruction:
One `fmt.Println()` = one line of output. Want two lines? Use two.

Think of it like texting — each `Println` is a separate message bubble.

Add a second `fmt.Println("something")` below the first one.
hint: Add fmt.Println("something") on a new line inside the curly braces, right after the first one.

codeChecks.message: Add a second fmt.Println() line below the first.

successMessage: Two messages, two lines. The computer reads your code top-to-bottom, like a recipe. First this, then that.

title: Go big — three lines
instruction:
Let's go bigger. Three `fmt.Println()` calls, three lines of output.

Make each one say something different. Get creative — this is your program.
hint: You need three separate fmt.Println() lines inside the curly braces.
successMessage: Three instructions, executed in order. Every program you'll ever write works this way — step by step, top to bottom.

title: The front door: func main()
instruction:
Every Go program has a `func main()`. It's the front door — Go walks in, finds `main`, and starts running whatever's inside the `{ }`.

No `main` = no program. Go would just stare at your code and do nothing.

Run this and watch the order of the output.
starter:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Step 1: wake up")
	fmt.Println("Step 2: coffee")
	fmt.Println("Step 3: code")
}
successMessage: See how it runs in order? func main() is just saying: 'Start here, do these things.' Every Go program you write will have one.

title: The toolbox: import
instruction:
`import "fmt"` loads a built-in toolbox called **fmt** (short for "format").

Without it, `fmt.Println` doesn't exist — it's like trying to use a drill without plugging it in.

You already have two lines. Add a third `fmt.Println()` and run it.
hint: Add fmt.Println("your text") on a new line inside the curly braces.
successMessage: Go has dozens of built-in toolboxes — fmt, math, strings, net/http. You just used your first one.

title: Bug hunt: round 1 🔍
instruction:
Time to play detective. This code has a bug — it won't run.

Press Run, read the error message (it's actually helpful!), then fix the code.

*Hint: count the quote marks.*
hint: The string is missing a closing quote. Add " at the end of "Go is great".
successMessage: You just debugged your first program. Reading error messages and fixing things is 80% of a real developer's day. Seriously.

title: Bug hunt: round 2 🔍
instruction:
Another bug, different flavor.

Run it, read the error, and fix it. Go is picky about something here...
hint: Go is case-sensitive. `println` (lowercase p) is not the same as `Println` (uppercase P).
successMessage: Go is case-sensitive — `Println` works, `println` doesn't. In Go, if a name starts with a capital letter, it's "exported" (public). Lowercase = private. You'll see why this matters later.

title: Write it from scratch ✍️
instruction:
No training wheels this time. The `main` block is empty.

Write three `fmt.Println()` lines and make the program do something. Anything you want.
hint: Type three fmt.Println("something") lines between the curly braces.

codeChecks.message: Add three fmt.Println() lines inside the curly braces.

successMessage: You wrote a Go program from a blank canvas. That's the real thing — no copy-paste, no hand-holding. You're a programmer now.

title: Chapter 1 complete! 🎉
instruction:
Let's recap what you just learned:

• Every Go program has `package main` and `func main()`
• `import "fmt"` loads the printing toolbox
• `fmt.Println()` prints text to the screen
• Go runs code top-to-bottom
• Go is case-sensitive (capital letters matter!)

Change the message to `"Chapter 1 complete"` and run it to finish this tutorial.
hint: Replace the text inside the quotes with exactly: Chapter 1 complete
successMessage: You're officially a Go programmer. Next up: variables — where things get really interesting. Your programs are about to get a brain. 🧠
